stem: A curve is given parametrically by \( x(t)=[[a]]t^{2}+[[b]]t+[[c]] \), \( y(t)=t \). Find the \( x \)-coordinate of the point on the curve at \( t=[[t0]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to square \( t\) in the leading term.
This flips the sign of the linear term.
This drops the constant term \( c\) entirely.
This reports \( x'(t_0)\), the derivative, instead of \( x(t_0)\) itself.
This is off by one from the correct \( x\)-coordinate.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct \( x\)-coordinate.

stem: A curve is given parametrically by \( x(t)=t \), \( y(t)=[[a]]\sin t+[[b]] \). Find the \( y \)-coordinate of the point on the curve at \( t=\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{[[n]]} \).
solution: Substituting \( t=\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{[[n]]} \) into \( y(t)=[[a]]\sin t+[[b]] \) gives \( y=[[a]]\sin\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{[[n]]}+[[b]]=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( \cos t\) instead of \( \sin t\).
This subtracts the constant \( b\) instead of adding it.
This has the wrong sign on the sine term.
This drops the constant shift \( b\) entirely.
This is off by one from the correct \( y\)-coordinate.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct \( y\)-coordinate.

stem: A particle moves along the curve \( x(t)=[[p]]t \), \( y(t)=[[q]]t \) for \( [[t0]]\le t\le [[t1]] \). Find the total arc length traveled.
solution: The speed is \( \sqrt{x'(t)^{2}+y'(t)^{2}}=\sqrt{[[p]]^{2}+[[q]]^{2}} \), constant in \( t \), so the arc length is this speed times the elapsed time: \( \sqrt{[[p]]^{2}+[[q]]^{2}}\cdot([[t1]]-[[t0]])=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the sum of the endpoints instead of their difference (the elapsed time).
This adds the velocity components instead of combining them via the Pythagorean theorem.
This subtracts the squared components under the root instead of adding them.
This forgets to multiply by the elapsed time entirely.
This is off by one from the correct arc length.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct arc length.

stem: A curve is given parametrically by \( x(t)=[[a]]t^{2} \), \( y(t)=[[b]]t^{3} \) for \( 0\le t\le [[t1]] \). Find the arc length of the curve over this interval.
solution: The speed is \( \sqrt{x'(t)^{2}+y'(t)^{2}}=\sqrt{(2\cdot[[a]]t)^{2}+(3\cdot[[b]]t^{2})^{2}} \); integrating from \( 0 \) to \( [[t1]] \) via the substitution \( u=4\cdot[[a]]^{2}+9\cdot[[b]]^{2}t^{2}\) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds a stray unit to the correct antiderivative value.
This evaluates the integrand only at the endpoint and multiplies by \( t_1\) instead of integrating it.
This uses the average of the endpoint speed and the initial speed multiplied by \( t_1\), rather than integrating.
This mistakes the parametrization itself, \( x(t_1)+y(t_1)\)-style, for the arc length.
This subtracts a stray unit from the correct antiderivative value.
This adds a small fractional error to the correct antiderivative value.
